one_line_takeaway: Batch-Norm mean/variance statistics across network depths form a 'characteristic vector' that detects clean-label poisons by their convergence toward the target class, cutting attack success to ~3.7% while preserving accuracy where KNN/Spectral fail on strong attacks and EPIC wrecks accuracy.
key_finding: On CIFAR10 transfer learning the defense cuts average attack success rate to 3.67% (vs 100% undefended) with essentially no test-accuracy loss (~89.5% vs 89.64% clean). It sharply outperforms KNN and Spectral on the stronger BP and GM attacks: for BP, average ASR is 4.17% (Ours) vs 66.05% (KNN) and 55.37% (Spectral); for GM, 3.95% (Ours) vs 47.73% (KNN) and 66.04% (Spectral). Spectral also badly degrades test accuracy under BP/GM (down to ~65-67%). EPIC drives ASR to 0% but at a heavy accuracy cost (test accuracy collapses to ~70% at low medoid settings and ~15-16% at the 0.3 setting), whereas the proposed method preserves ~89.4% accuracy. Results hold in the harder cross-distribution CINIC10->CIFAR10 setting (Table 3: Ours FC 0.00% ASR/87.65% acc, BP 4.44%/87.49%, GM 2.22%/87.49%, vs clean acc 87.83%).
core_contribution: The paper proposes a sanitization (filtering) defense against triggerless clean-label data-poisoning attacks in the transfer-learning setting. Its own novel contribution is a 'characteristic vector' representation of each datapoint, built from the channel-wise mean and variance of every Batch Normalization (BN) layer of a frozen pre-trained feature extractor, capturing low- and high-level feature-map statistics at different network depths. For each class the authors compute a centroid 'pseudo-datapoint' characteristic vector, then relabel each training point to the class whose centroid it is closest to (Eq. 6/8, cosine-distance-based, weighted per BN layer by gamma and mean-vs-variance by beta). Any point whose predicted label differs from its dataset label is flagged as a poison and removed. The key insight distinguishing this from prior feature-space defenses (KNN/Spectral) is that it measures a poison's CONVERGENCE toward the target class (which any successful attack must induce) rather than its deviation from the base class, making it robust to large poison/perturbation budgets and generalizable across attack families. The paper also empirically shows that undetected poisons are 'failed poisons' that do not actually shift the decision boundary, and that real poisons land in the target-class manifold in characteristic-vector space.
threat_model: Standard transfer-learning setting: victim uses a pre-trained model phi as a frozen feature extractor to fine-tune a downstream classifier f on a related task. Adversary mounts a triggerless clean-label poisoning attack (integrity violation): it cannot insert or delete training points but can apply an l-infinity-constrained perturbation to a subset P of training points of a chosen base class y_b, so that a specific target sample x_t (not in the training set) is misclassified as y_b at inference, with unaltered labels and no inference-time trigger. The paper assumes the STRONGEST possible attacker: full knowledge of the training data, training procedure, and feature extractor phi. The defender (victim) has no knowledge of the target sample, base/target class, poison budget, or perturbation budget, and has no access to any known-clean reference data beyond the (possibly poisoned) training set.
limitations: Explicitly stated: the method REQUIRES a pre-trained feature extractor to build characteristic vectors, so it only applies to the transfer-learning setting and not train-from-scratch (future work proposes an iterative training extension). Other observable limits: evaluation is confined to small 32x32 image datasets (CIFAR10/CINIC10) and CNNs with BN layers, so applicability to BN-free architectures (e.g., transformers) or other modalities is untested; it targets triggerless clean-label integrity attacks only, not backdoor/trigger or availability/privacy poisoning; effectiveness depends on a 'robustly trained feature extractor'; the CIFAR10 setting reuses the extractor's own distribution, which the authors themselves note is not truly representative transfer learning; and hyperparameters gamma (per-layer weight) and beta (mean-vs-variance weight) are introduced but their tuning/sensitivity is not detailed in the main text.

datasets_used: CIFAR10 (60,000 32x32 color images, 10 classes; 50k train / 10k test)
CINIC10 (270,000 32x32 images, superset of CIFAR10 with downsampled ImageNet images; train/val/test 90k each; used as different-but-similar distribution for cross-distribution transfer)
quantitative_results: CIFAR10: overall average attack success rate reduced to 3.67% (Ours) vs 100% undefended
CIFAR10 BP average ASR: Ours 4.17% vs KNN 66.05% vs Spectral 55.37%
CIFAR10 GM average ASR: Ours 3.95% vs KNN 47.73% vs Spectral 66.04%
CIFAR10 FC average ASR: Ours 2.90% vs KNN 8.39% vs Spectral 2.87%
CIFAR10 test accuracy: Ours averages ~89.5-89.59% vs clean 89.64%; Spectral degrades to 66.99% (BP) and 64.58% (GM)
EPIC drives ASR to 0% but test accuracy falls to ~70-73% (medoid 0.1) and ~15.71-16.38% (medoid 0.3 SGD), vs Ours ~89.37-89.39%
CINIC10->CIFAR10 (Table 3, 14% budget): Ours FC 0.00% ASR/87.65% acc, BP 4.44%/87.49%, GM 2.22%/87.49%; clean acc 87.83%
CINIC10 KNN fails on BP (82.22% ASR) and GM (35.71%); Spectral fails on BP (75.56%) and GM (76.40%)
Poison budget 14% of dataset; perturbation budgets 10/255 to 30/255; distribution analysis used 200 poisoned + 200 clean characteristic vectors
baselines_compared: Deep-KNN (Peri et al., feature-space clustering defense)
Spectral Signatures (Tran et al., top-singular-vector correlation; recent Fowl et al. implementation)
EPIC (Yang et al., gradient-space isolated-point filtering; current SOTA), tested at medoid settings 0.1/0.2/0.3 with Adam and SGD
